explanation: The requirement states that the self-test procedure should prop_0. This implies that 'prop_0' should eventually happen at some time during execution. In LTL, this is expressed as 'eventually prop_0', or F prop_0.
Whenever prop_0 holds, it must be the case that prop_1 holds at the same time, i.e., whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is also true.
The requirement is that the self-test procedure must satisfy 'prop_0' at some point in time. In LTL, 'eventually prop_0' expresses that prop_0 will occur at some future point.
This requires that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must already be true, i.e., prop_0 is always preceded or accompanied by prop_1.

The requirement states that the self-test procedure must 'prop_0'. Assuming this means 'prop_0' must eventually happen (i.e., be true at some point), the LTL formula expressing this is 'eventually prop_0', using the 'F' (finally/eventually) operator.

The requirement 'The self-test procedure shall prop_0' indicates that prop_0 must hold (i.e., be true) at some point. In LTL, the standard way to specify that something must happen eventually is to use the F (eventually) operator.
The requirement states that the self-test procedure shall result in prop_0. This can be represented as 'eventually prop_0' (F prop_0), meaning at some point in the future, prop_0 becomes true.

The requirement states that the self-test procedure shall 'prop_0'. Interpreting this as a requirement that 'prop_0' must always hold throughout the system execution, we use the 'globally' (G) operator applied to prop_0.

The requirement states that the self-test procedure shall 'prop_0.' In LTL, to ensure that something must eventually occur (i.e., it is required that it happens at least once in the future), we use the 'eventually' operator F. Thus, the LTL formula is F prop_0.
The formula simply asserts that 'prop_0' holds at the current time.
